title: The Web's Birthday: A Digital Revolution Begins
story: Imagine a world without websites. That changed when two clever scientists had a big idea. Tim Berners-Lee and Robert Cailliau, working at CERN in Switzerland, proposed the World Wide Web in 1990. On November 12, they shared their plan to connect information across computers globally. This invention would transform how we learn, work, and communicate, sparking a digital revolution that continues today.
activity_question: Where did Tim Berners-Lee and Robert Cailliau work when they proposed the World Wide Web?
follow_up_question: How do you think the World Wide Web has changed the way we live since 1990?

title: Tiny Robot Lands on a Speeding Comet
story: Zoom! A robot the size of a washing machine made history in 2014. On November 12, the Philae probe touched down on a comet racing through space. This brave little explorer hitched a ride on the Rosetta spacecraft, launched by the European Space Agency. After a decade-long journey, Philae became the first human-made object to land on a comet's nucleus. It was like hitting a bullseye on a target moving faster than a speeding bullet!

title: Voyager I's Close Encounter with Saturn
story: Zooming through space, the Voyager I probe made history in 1980. On November 12, it swooped incredibly close to Saturn, coming within about 77,000 miles of the ringed planet. That's like flying from Earth to the Moon and back over a dozen times! This daring flyby gave scientists amazing new views of Saturn's swirling clouds, mysterious rings, and icy moons.

title: Welton Taylor: Scientist and Civil Rights Hero
story: Welton Taylor was born in Birmingham, Alabama in 1919. He grew up to become a brilliant microbiologist and inventor who made food safer for everyone. Taylor created special tests to find harmful bacteria like salmonella. He also fought for equal rights, showing that science and social justice can go hand in hand.

title: A Doctor's Brief Baseball Dream
story: Archibald Graham stepped onto a Major League field for just one game in 1905. Born on November 12, 1876, he became known as 'Moonlight' Graham. His fleeting baseball career and later work as a doctor inspired the movie 'Field of Dreams.' Graham's real-life journey from a single MLB appearance to a respected physician captured imaginations far beyond the baseball diamond.

follow_up_question: How many Major League Baseball games did Graham play in his career?

title: American Chemist Makes Nobel History
story: Theodore W. Richards made history with his groundbreaking work in chemistry. In 1915, on November 12, he became the first American to win the Nobel Prize in Chemistry. Richards' precise measurements of atomic weights helped scientists better understand the building blocks of our world. His achievement opened doors for future American scientists in the field.

follow_up_question: Why are atomic weights important in chemistry?

title: Boom! The Explosive Fire Fighter
story: Kaboom! In 1723, Ambrose Godfrey had a blast with fire safety. This clever inventor patented a wild new way to fight flames on November 12. His secret weapon? Gunpowder! Godfrey's system used small explosions to spread fire-dousing liquid quickly. While it sounds dangerous, this fiery idea sparked new thinking about battling blazes.
activity_question: What did Ambrose Godfrey use in his fire extinguisher system?
follow_up_question: Why do you think using explosions to fight fire might be risky?
